title: CP 04: Rates of hydrolysis of halogenoalkanes

cards.side1: Why is ethanol used as a solvent in the hydrolysis of halogenoalkanes?
cards.side2: Halogenoalkanes and aqueous silver nitrate are immiscible. Ethanol acts as a common solvent that allows them to mix and react.

cards.side1: How do you ensure a fair test when comparing the rate of hydrolysis of different halogenoalkanes?
cards.side2: Use a water bath to keep the temperature constant, as temperature affects the rate of reaction. Use the same volumes and concentrations of reagents.

cards.side1: What is the expected observation when a chloroalkane reacts with aqueous silver nitrate?
cards.side2: A white precipitate of silver chloride ($\mathrm{AgCl}$) forms slowly.

cards.side1: What is the expected order of reaction rates for the hydrolysis of 1-chlorobutane, 1-bromobutane, and 1-iodobutane?
cards.side2: 1-iodobutane (fastest) > 1-bromobutane > 1-chlorobutane (slowest). This is because the C-I bond is the weakest and breaks most easily.

cards.side1: What are the safety considerations when heating halogenoalkanes?
cards.side2: Halogenoalkanes are toxic and flammable. Avoid inhalation by using a fume cupboard or well-ventilated room, and keep away from naked flames (use a water or sand bath).

cards.side1: What is a major source of error when timing the appearance of the precipitate in CP 04?
cards.side2: The subjective judgment of exactly when the precipitate becomes visible, which varies between individuals.

cards.side1: How can the methodology be improved to make the timing of the precipitate more objective?
cards.side2: Use a colorimeter to measure the decrease in light transmittance as the precipitate forms, rather than relying on the naked eye.

cards.side1: What common student error relates to the temperature of the reagents before mixing?
cards.side2: Failing to allow the separate tubes of halogenoalkane and silver nitrate to reach thermal equilibrium in the water bath before mixing them.
